### Extensional Semantics for L$_S$

Recall that the language L$_S$ countenances four distinct sentence forms:

$$
\begin{align*}
\all{A}{B} &\qquad\qquad(\textrm{All $A$s are $B$s})\\
\no{A}{B} &\qquad\qquad(\textrm{No $A$s are $B$s})\\
\some{A}{B} &\qquad\qquad(\textrm{Some $A$s is $B$s})\\
\somenot{A}{B} &\qquad\qquad(\textrm{Some $A$s are not $B$s})\\
\end{align*}
$$

Which are the logical and which the non-logical elements of the language?
As suggested above, the non-logical elements of the language are the terms.
Their meanings will be allowed to vary from one interpretation to the next.

But what does it means to interpret these terms? Here we will adopt the approach
of interpreting the terms 'extensionally'. Terms in the language L$_S$ are meant
to stand for properties expressed by common noun phrases, such as 'animal',
'cold-blooded', or 'green'. The extension of a common noun phrase is the set of
all individual objects that possess the property denoted by that phrase. So, for
example, the extension of the term 'cat' is the set of all cats, the extension
of 'cold-blooded' the set of all cold-blooded creatures.[^intension] Thus, in an
extensional semantics, a term will be interpreted as a set of objects, the
members of which are all and only those objects possessing the property denoted
by the term.

[^intension]:
    By contrast, the _intension_ of a common noun phrase is the
    meaning of the phrase, or that which determines its extension. Thus, for
    example, while 'human' and 'featherless biped' have the same extension since
    every human is a featherless biped and vice versa, they have different
    intensions since the two phrases differ in meaning. For a more detailed
    discussion of the distinction between extension and intension, see
    @Carnap1988-nm, ch. 1.

But what exactly do we mean by an _object_? For semantics purposes, we need not
specify what an object is. An object is simply a member of a certain fixed set
which we will refer to as the 'universe' and which we will denote by $\W$. We
may assume that $\W$ is very big, we may even assume that it is infinite. This
universe of objects will serve as the background domain for our interpretations.

To interpret the language is simply to assign to every term an extension,
that is, a subset of objects in the universe.

::: {#def-interpretation-Ls}
An _interpretation_ of the language $\Ls$ is a
function assigning to each term a subset of $\W$. If $\si$ is an interpretation
and $A$ is a term, we write $\si(A)$ for the subset of $\W$ assigned to $A$ by the
interpretation $\si$.  
:::

:::{#fig-model-S}
![](/content/static/images/syllogistic-model.svg){width=450 fig-align="center" fig-alt="Syllogistic model"}

An extensional interpretation of the language $\Ls$.
:::

On any given interpretation of the language, every term is assigned a specific
extension, that is, a specific set of objects in the universe. To complete our
specification of the semantics for the language we must explain how it is that
the truth or falsity of any sentence in the language, gets decided on a given
interpretation. In doing so, we will, in effect, be providing an account of the
intended meanings of the logical elements of the syntax, in this case the
copulas $\all{}{}, \no{}{}, \some{}{}$, and $\somenot{}{}$.

How then do we go from an interpretation of the
language, say $\si$, to a specific truth valuation of the language, call it
$v^\si$. Let's start with sentences of the form $\all{A}{B}$. On the
intepretation $\si$, the extension of $A$ is given by the set $\si(A)$ and the
extension of $B$ by the set $\si(B)$. Under what conditions should the sentence
$\all{A}{B}$ be judged to be true? Since $\all{A}{B}$ is intended to express the
fact that all $A$s are $B$s, $\all{A}{B}$ should be true just in case every
object in $\si(A)$ is in $\si(B)$. That is:

$$
\textrm{$v^\si(\all{A}{B})=\true$ iff $\si(A)\subset \si(B)$}
$$

What about sentences of the form $\no{A}{B}$? This sentence is meant to express
the fact that no $A$s are $B$s and so it should be true, on the interpretation $\si$,
just in case $\si(A)$ and $\si(B)$ do not overlap, i.e.:

$$
\textrm{$v^\si(\no{A}{B}) = \true$ iff $\si(A)\cap\si(B)=\emptyset$}
$$

The corresponding rules explaining how to assess the truth and falsity of sentences
of the form $\some{A}{B}$ and $\somenot{A}{B}$ can be stated as follows:

$$
\textrm{$v^\si(\some{A}{B}) = \true$ iff $\si(A)\cap\si(B) \neq \emptyset$}
$$

$$
\textrm{$v^\si(\somenot{A}{B}) = \true$ iff $\si(A)-\si(B)\neq \emptyset$}
$$

To illustrate the application of these semantic rules, let $\si$ be the intepretation partially specified in @fig-model-S above. On this interpretation, we have, for example:

$$
\begin{align*}
v^\si(\all{D}{C})=\true \\
v^\si(\some{A}{B})=\false \\
v^\si(\no{C}{A})=\false \\
v^\si(\somenot{D}{B})=\true
\end{align*}
$$

Now, let's return to the issue of argumentative validity. We had previously defined a valid
argument as one for which there is no admissible truth valuation under which all
of the premises are true but the conclusion is false. Now we are in a position
to answer the question of what counts as an admissible truth valuation.

The class of admissible truth valuations with respect to which we will assess
the validity of arguments will be those of the form $v^\si$, where $\si$ is an
intepretation of the language. In other words, an admissible truth valuation is
one which results from the application of the semantic rules to a specific
assignment of extensions to the terms. We can now update our
definition of argumentative validity as follows:

::: {#def-validity}
The argument

$$
\Ga\proves\ph
$$

is _valid_ if, for every interpretation $\si$, the following is true: if $v^\si(\psi)=\true$, for all $\psi\in\Ga$, then $v^\si(\ph)=\true$.
:::

To express that the argument $\Ga\proves\ph$ is valid, we write:

$$
\Ga\models\ph
$$

In this case, we may also say that $\Ga$ _entails_ $\ph$, or that $\ph$ is a _logical consequence_ of $\Ga$.

In general, the semantic rules for the language---which capture the meanings of
the logical elements of the language---will be chosen in such a way that not every
truth valuation will be of the form $v^\sigma$. Hence, it is these semantic
rules that will render our standard of validity nontrivial, in the sense that it
will admit as valid not just circular arguments. In the case of L$_S$, for
example, we may observe that there is no admissible truth valuation which
assigns $\true$ to both the sentences $\all{A}{B}$ and $\somenot{A}{B}$. To see
why, let $\si$ be any interpretation of the language and suppose that
$v^\si(\all{A}{B})=\true$. Then, by our semantic rule for the $\all{}{}$ copula,
we have $\si(A)\subset\si(B)$. But then $\si(A)-\si(B)=\emptyset$, and so, by
our semantic rule for the $\somenot{}{}$ copula, $v^\si(\somenot{A}{B})=\false$.

Thus, on our extensional semantics $\all{A}{B}$ and $\somenot{A}{B}$ cannot both
be true. In fact, it is easy to verify that they also cannot both be false. In
other words, the one is true just in case the other is false. And the same relation
obtains between the propositions $\no{A}{B}$ and $\some{A}{B}$

:::{#exr-e-i-contrariety .exercise}
{{< include /content/exercises/e-i-contrariety.qmd >}}
:::

Now, at last, we have in place the necessary resources to make precise our
initial, somewhat vague intuition that the following argument is valid:

$$
\frac{\textrm{All humans are mammals}\hspace{2mm},\hspace{2mm}\textrm{No mammals are
cold-blooded}}{\textrm{No humans are cold-blooded}}
$$

Let $A$ stand for 'human', $B$ for 'mammal' and $C$ for 'cold-blooded'. Then,
translated into our formal language L$_S$, the claim that this argument is valid
is expressed:

$$
\all{A}{B}, \no{B}{C}\models\no{A}{C}
$$

To confirm this, we must show that, for any interpretation, if
the premises are true, the conclusion is true as well. Let $\si$ be an
interpretation such that $v^\si(\all{A}{B}) = \true$ and
$v^\si(\no{B}{C})=\true$. Then, from the first of these claims, we have
$\si(A)\subset\si(B)$, and, from the second, we have
$\si(B)\inter\si(C)=\emptyset$. Now, suppose $x\in\si(A)$. Then, since
$\si(A)\subset\si(B)$, $x\in \si(B)$. But since $\si(B)\inter\si(C)=\emptyset$,
it follows that $x\not\in\si(C)$. So, for any object $x$, if $x\in\si(A)$ it follows that
$x\not\in\si(C)$. But this means that $\si(A)\inter\si(C)=\emptyset$. So, by our
semantic rules, $v^\si(\no{A}{C})=\true$. Hence, the argument is valid.

Now, let's consider another argument:

$$
\frac{\textrm{No humans are mammals}\hspace{2mm},\hspace{2mm}\textrm{All mammals are
cold-blooded}}{\textrm{No humans are cold-blooded}}
$$

This argument can be formalized in the language L$_S$ as follows: $\no{A}{B},
\all{B}{C}\proves \no{A}{C}$. Is this argument valid? To show that it is not
valid it suffices to provide a counterexample to the argument. This is an
interpretation under which the premises are true but the conclusion is false.
Let $a$, $b$, and $c$ be any three distinct objects in $\W$, and let $\si$ be an
interpretation satisfying:

$$
\si(A) = \{a, b\}
$$

$$
\si(B) = \{c\}
$$

$$
\si(C) = \{b, c\}
$$

It is easy to verify that the interpretation $\si$ is a counterexample to the
argument, for, on this interpretation, the premises of the argument are true
and its conclusion is false:

$$
\si(A)\inter\si(B) = \{a, b\}\inter\{c\} = \emptyset \hspace{3mm}\Rightarrow\hspace{3mm}
v^\si(\no{A}{B}) = \true
$$

$$
\si(B) = \{c\} \subset \{b,c\} = \si(C) \hspace{3mm}\Rightarrow\hspace{3mm}
v^\si(\all{B}{C}) = \true
$$

$$
\si(A)\inter\si(C) = \{a, b\}\inter\{b, c\} \neq \emptyset \hspace{3mm}\Rightarrow\hspace{3mm}
v^\si(\no{A}{C}) = \false
$$

We can visually represent this counterexample as follows:

![](/content/static/images/syllogism-counterexample.svg){width=450 fig-align="center" fig-alt="Syllogistic counterexample"}

:::{#exr-syllogisms-validity .exercise}
{{< include /content/exercises/syllogisms-validity.qmd >}}
:::
